query: What is the mean of the standard normal distribution Z ~ N(0, 1)?
What is the standard deviation of the standard normal distribution?
According to the Empirical Rule, approximately what percentage of x values lie within one standard deviation of the mean in a normal distribution?
According to the Empirical Rule, approximately what percentage of x values lie within two standard deviations of the mean?
According to the Empirical Rule, approximately what percentage of x values lie within three standard deviations of the mean?
What are the z-scores corresponding to ±1 standard deviation from the mean in a standard normal distribution?
What are the z-scores corresponding to ±2 standard deviations from the mean in a standard normal distribution?
What are the z-scores corresponding to ±3 standard deviations from the mean in a standard normal distribution?
If X ~ N(5, 6) and x = 17, what is the z-score?
If X ~ N(5, 6) and x = 1, what is the z-score (rounded to two decimal places)?
What is the formula for calculating a z-score from a value x in a normal distribution with mean μ and standard deviation σ?
What formula is used to find the kth percentile of X when the z-score is known?
In a normal distribution with mean 63 and standard deviation 5, what is the probability that a randomly selected value is greater than 65?
In the third exam/final exam example, what is the equation of the least-squares regression line?
In the third exam/final exam example, what is the value of the correlation coefficient r?
In the third exam/final exam example, what is the coefficient of determination r²?
In the third exam/final exam example, how many data points (n) were used?
What is the p-value for the significance test of the correlation coefficient in the third exam/final exam example?
What significance level α is used throughout Chapter 12 for hypothesis testing of the correlation coefficient?
What is the formula for the correlation coefficient r?
What is the range of possible values for the correlation coefficient r?
What does r = 0 indicate about the linear relationship between two variables?
What does r = 1 indicate about the linear relationship between two variables?
What does r = –1 indicate about the linear relationship between two variables?
In the linear regression equation ŷ = a + bx, what does the slope b describe?
In the linear regression equation y = a + bx, what does a represent geometrically?
What is the formula for the slope b of the least-squares regression line in terms of correlation coefficient r, sy, and sx?
What important point does the best-fit line always pass through?
What is the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) minimized to achieve in linear regression?
What is the formula for the standard deviation of the residuals s in terms of SSE and n?
In the third exam/final exam example with the outlier removed, what is the new equation of the best-fit line?
In the third exam/final exam example with the outlier removed, what is the new correlation coefficient r?
In the 2012 SAT verbal section, what was the mean score μ?
In the 2012 SAT verbal section, what was the standard deviation σ?
What were the mean and standard deviation of heights for 15–18-year-old males from Chile in 2009–2010?
For the IQ distribution, what are the mean and standard deviation given in the textbook?
In the checkout line wait time example (Thuy Dau et al.), what was the sample mean calculated?
In the checkout line wait time example, what was the sample standard deviation?
In the NUMMI assembly line example, what was the mean number of defective cars per sample of n=100?
In the NUMMI example, what is the standard deviation of defective cars per 100?
For the CD player lifetime example, what is the mean lifetime?
For the CD player lifetime example, what is the standard deviation?
What is the 90th percentile of the final exam score distribution with mean 63 and standard deviation 5?
What is the 70th percentile of the final exam score distribution with mean 63 and standard deviation 5?
In the third exam/final exam example, which data point was identified as an outlier?
What is the standard deviation s of the residuals in the third exam/final exam example (before removing the outlier)?
In a normal distribution, if the area to the left of x is 0.0228, what is the area to the right?
What is the 80th percentile of a normal distribution with mean 36.9 and standard deviation 13.9?
For the IQR of smartphone user ages (mean 36.9, sd 13.9), what is Q3?
For the IQR of smartphone user ages (mean 36.9, sd 13.9), what is Q1?
In the coin flip example (n=100, p=0.20), what are the mean and standard deviation of the number of heads?
What is the formula for the test statistic t used in testing significance of the correlation coefficient?
For the Sports Stadiums data in Chapter 6, what was the sample mean maximum capacity?
In the pregnancy length example, what is the mean number of days and standard deviation?
What is the probability that a randomly chosen mandarin orange has a diameter larger than 6.0 cm, given mean 5.85 cm and sd 0.24 cm?
For a normal distribution with mean 68 and standard deviation 3 (golf scores), what is the probability that a score is less than 65?
What is the 25th percentile of a normal distribution with mean 2 and standard deviation 0.5?
In the scratch-off lottery ticket example, what is the probability that a ticket wins?
For Facebook usage (mean 28%, sd 5%), what is the probability that the usage is at least 30%?
What is the 95th percentile of the Facebook usage distribution with mean 28 and standard deviation 5?
In the Consumer Price Index (CPI) regression example, what is the equation of the best-fit line?
In the CPI example, what is the correlation coefficient r?
In the CPI example, what is the estimated CPI for 1990?
What are the null and alternative hypotheses for testing the significance of the correlation coefficient?
In Chapter 12, what calculator function is used to find the least-squares regression line?
For df = 8, what are the critical values of the correlation coefficient at the 95% level?
For df = 12, what is the critical value of r at the 95% level?
What were the two variables used in the third exam/final exam example?
In the height vs. stories regression example (Chapter 12), how many buildings are in the dataset?
In the sparrow hawks study, how many colonies were tagged?
What percent of variation in the final exam grade is explained by the third exam score using the regression line?

What is the TI-83/84 calculator command to find cumulative normal probabilities?
What is the TI-83/84 calculator command to find a value at a given percentile?
For the fat calories distribution with mean 36 and standard deviation 10, what is the maximum value for the lower 25%?
What is the 80th percentile of the fly ball distance distribution (mean 250, sd 50)?
In the unemployment wait time (mean 3, sd 1.5), what is the probability of less than one hour unsupervised?
What is the Greek letter used to denote the population correlation coefficient?
What notation is used for a normal distribution with mean μ and standard deviation σ?
In Chapter 12, how many degrees of freedom are used in the t-test for the correlation coefficient with n data points?
In the final exam scores example (μ = 63, σ = 5), what is the probability of scoring less than 85?

What is the standard normal distribution, and how is it denoted?
Define the normal distribution and identify its two parameters.

What is the cumulative distribution function P(X < x) and how is it interpreted?
What does it mean for a normal distribution to be “symmetric”?

What is a critical value in the context of a normal distribution?

What is a linear equation in the statistical context, and how is it written?

What is 1 – r², and what does it represent?
What is a positive correlation, and what does it imply about the relationship between x and y?
What is a negative correlation, and what does it imply about the relationship between x and y?
What does the phrase “correlation does not imply causation” mean?
What is the population correlation coefficient ρ (rho)?
What is the null hypothesis H₀ in a test of significance for the correlation coefficient?
What is the alternative hypothesis Hₐ in a test of significance for the correlation coefficient?

What does it mean for a correlation coefficient to be “not significant”?
What are the assumptions required for testing the significance of the correlation coefficient?
What is interpolation in the context of linear regression prediction?
What is extrapolation in the context of linear regression prediction, and why is it unreliable?
What is an outlier in the context of regression analysis?
What is an influential point in linear regression, and how does it differ from an outlier?

What is the independent variable in a regression model?
What is the dependent variable in a regression model?
What is multivariate data, and how does it differ from bivariate data?
What is the Least Squares Criterion in linear regression?
What is the IQR (Interquartile Range) and how is it calculated?
What is a continuous random variable, and how does the normal distribution fit this definition?
What is the area under the entire normal distribution curve, and what does it represent?
What does it mean when we say the best-fit line “passes through the point (x̄, ȳ)”?
What is a linear relationship in a statistical context?
What is the form of the standard linear equation in statistics (as opposed to algebra)?
What is the LinRegTTest on the TI-83/84 calculator, and what output does it provide?
What is the invNorm function on a calculator, and when is it used?
What is normalcdf, and when is it used?
What is a “well-designed random sample” in the context of regression assumptions?
What is meant by “equal variance” in the regression assumptions?
Define the term “direction” of a relationship as shown in a scatter plot.
Define the term “strength” of a relationship as shown in a scatter plot.
What does the sign of r tell us about the direction of a linear relationship?
What is the coefficient of correlation, and who developed it?
What is the notation X ~ N(μ, σ), and what does each parameter represent?
What is the difference between σ (sigma) and s in the context of normal distributions and regression?
What is “regression to the mean,” and what concept in this chapter is related to it?
What does the slope b = r(sy/sx) mean in words?
What is meant by the “domain of observed x values” in regression prediction?
What is a “line of best fit” and why is it called the “best”?
Define a normal probability distribution in plain terms for a non-statistician.
What is a “standardized value” and how does standardization help compare different data sets?
What does it mean when the z-score of a value x is positive versus negative?
What is the bell curve, and in what fields is it commonly observed?
What is the 68-95-99.7 rule and why is it useful in practice?
Define the concept of “variation” in the context of the coefficient of determination r².
What does it mean for residuals to be “mutually independent” in regression?
In a regression context, what does it mean for y values to be “normally distributed about the line”?
What is meant by “degrees of freedom” in the t-test for correlation significance?
What is the relationship between the sign of the slope b and the sign of the correlation coefficient r?
Define a “random variable” X ~ N(μ, σ) in words.
What is the practical meaning of the y-intercept when x = 0 in a real-world regression model?
What is the difference between a sample correlation coefficient r and a population correlation coefficient ρ?
What is the significance level α = 0.05 and what decision rule does it create in hypothesis testing?
What does “reject the null hypothesis” mean in plain language in the context of correlation testing?
What does “fail to reject the null hypothesis” mean in plain language in the context of correlation testing?
What is a “95% Critical Value” table for r, and how is it used to determine significance?
What is the normal approximation and when is it considered appropriate to use?
What is a stratified sampling method, and where is it used in the lab activity in Chapter 6?
What is the Consumer Price Index (CPI), and how is it used in the regression example?
What is the difference between the normal distribution N(μ, σ) and the standard normal distribution Z ~ N(0, 1)?
How does a change in the mean μ affect the shape and position of the normal curve, compared to a change in the standard deviation σ?
Compare the interpretation of a positive z-score versus a negative z-score.
How does the Empirical Rule differ for the 68%, 95%, and 99.7% intervals in terms of the number of standard deviations from the mean?
Compare the area to the left of a value x [P(X < x)] with the area to the right [P(X > x)] in a normal distribution. What is their relationship?
What is the difference between interpolation and extrapolation in regression prediction, and which is considered more reliable?
How does the least-squares regression line change when an outlier is removed? Compare the r values before and after removal in the third exam/final exam example.
Compare the correlation coefficient r = 0.6631 (before removing the outlier) with r = 0.9121 (after removing it). What do these values tell us about the quality of each model?
What is the difference between an outlier and an influential point in regression?
Compare P(X < x) and P(X ≤ x) for a continuous distribution. Are they the same or different? Why?
How do the slope and y-intercept of a regression line differ in their interpretations?
Compare a scatter plot showing r = 0.9 vs. one showing r = 0.3. How would these look visually, and what do they mean statistically?
How does a regression line with a positive slope differ from one with a negative slope in terms of real-world interpretation?
Compare the coefficient of determination r² = 0.44 versus r² = 0.91. What do these percentages mean about the explanatory power of the models?
What are the similarities and differences between the p-value method and the critical value method for testing significance of r?
Compare the normal distribution and the empirical (observed) distribution of data in a sample. Why might they differ?
How does the mean differ from the median in a perfectly symmetric normal distribution? What about in a skewed distribution?
Compare the behavior of the normal curve when σ is large versus when σ is small. How does this affect the spread and height of the curve?
How does the z-score for x = 17 in X ~ N(5, 6) compare to the z-score for y = 4 in Y ~ N(2, 1)? What does this tell us about standardization?
Compare the regression equation before and after removing the outlier (65, 175) from the third exam/final exam data. How does the slope change?
What is the difference between bivariate data and multivariate data? Give examples from the textbook for each.
Compare the roles of the independent variable (x) and the dependent variable (y) in a linear regression model.
How does the standard deviation of residuals s differ from the standard deviation of the original y values?
Compare the null hypothesis (H₀: ρ = 0) and the alternative hypothesis (Hₐ: ρ ≠ 0) in words about what each claims about the population.
Compare the IQR calculated from the theoretical normal distribution vs. the IQR calculated empirically from sample data. Why might these differ?
How does the least-squares regression line for the CPI data (r = 0.8694) compare in predictive quality to the third exam/final exam line (r = 0.6631)?
Compare the use of a scatter plot versus the correlation coefficient r for assessing the linear relationship between two variables. What can each tell you that the other cannot?
What is the difference between a data point that lies above the regression line versus one that lies below it? What does the sign of the residual tell us?
Compare the normal distribution curve to a uniform distribution in terms of shape, mean, and probability calculation.

How does the percentage of x values between the 1st and 2nd standard deviations (~27%) compare to the percentage between the 2nd and 3rd standard deviations (~4.7%)? What does this tell us about extreme values?
Compare the prediction accuracy of a regression model with r² = 0.72 vs. one with r² = 0.20. How confident should you be in each model’s predictions?
How does the standard normal distribution (Z) differ from a general normal distribution X ~ N(μ, σ) in terms of how probabilities are calculated?
Compare what the slope means in the Aaron’s Word Processing Service equation vs. the Svetlana tutoring equation. How is the real-world meaning of slope different?
How does the 25th percentile (Q1) compare to the 75th percentile (Q3) in a symmetric normal distribution? What is their relationship to the mean?
Compare the SAT score of 700 (μ=514, σ=117) to the ACT score of 30 (μ=21, σ=5.3) using z-scores. Who performed better relative to their test?
Compare the visual appearance of a scatter plot with a strong positive correlation vs. one with zero correlation. How do the points differ?
How does the value of the test statistic t in correlation testing relate to the size of the correlation r and the sample size n?
What is the difference between using r (correlation coefficient) and r² (coefficient of determination) to evaluate a regression model?
Compare P(X > x) = 1 – P(X < x) with the complementary rule in general probability. How does the normal distribution make this calculation especially straightforward?
How does the regression line prediction at x = 73 differ before and after removing the outlier (65,175) in the third exam/final exam example?

Compare the practical interpretation of the slope in a real-world regression (e.g., “for each additional story, height increases by ~13 feet”) with the mathematical definition of slope as “rise over run.”
In the sparrow hawk study, how does the regression equation for percent return vs. percent new birds compare to what an ecologist might intuitively expect? Does the data support this intuition?
Compare the effect of a large sample size vs. a small sample size on the reliability of using the correlation coefficient r for prediction.
How does a regression line with a slope close to 0 differ from one with a steep slope, in terms of what it tells us about the relationship between x and y?
Compare the probabilities calculated using the theoretical normal distribution to those calculated from cumulative relative frequencies in the checkout line example. Why are they not exactly equal?
How does the normal distribution compare to the bell curve approximations used for binomial distributions (as in the coin flip and NUMMI examples)?
Compare the concept of a “percentile” in a normal distribution to what it means in everyday language (e.g., “the 90th percentile student”).
What is the difference between using s (standard deviation of residuals) and 2s as a cutoff for identifying outliers in regression analysis?
Compare the use of LinRegTTest and the critical values table as two methods for testing significance of r. Under what circumstances might one be preferred over the other?
Compare the interpretation of ρ = 0 versus ρ ≠ 0 in terms of what they imply about the population relationship.
How does the meaning of the correlation coefficient r differ between r = 0.5 and r = –0.5? Are they equally strong? In what way do they differ?
Compare the regression equation and the scatter plot as tools for predicting y from x. When is each most useful?

In the normal distribution context, compare finding a probability P(X < x) to finding a percentile (given a probability, find x). How do these two types of problems differ in their computational approach?
How does removing an outlier from the data change the SSE (Sum of Squared Errors)? Should it increase or decrease? Why?
Compare the slope of the CPI regression line (b = 1.662) to the slope of the third exam/final exam line (b = 4.83). What are the different real-world meanings of these slopes?
Compare the regression analysis of the mandarin orange data to the regression of the sports stadium data. In both cases, how does the distribution of the data affect the reliability of predictions?
How does the concept of a “residual” in regression compare to the concept of a “deviation from the mean” in descriptive statistics?
Compare the Empirical Rule as applied to a distribution of exam scores versus a distribution of heights. How does the practical interpretation change even though the statistical rule is the same?
Compare what it means when the sign of r is positive vs. when it is negative in the context of physical measurements (e.g., height vs. weight, or car weight vs. fuel efficiency).
In the regression assumptions, compare the assumption of “normality of y values” to the assumption of “equal variance.” What would a violation of each look like on a scatter plot?
How does the meaning of “r is not significant” (insufficient evidence) differ from “r = 0” (no correlation)? Are they the same conclusion?
Compare the effect of having n = 5 data points vs. n = 100 data points on the critical value for r at the 95% confidence level. What does this tell us about sample size and significance?

Compare the regression line used for the sparrow hawk data (percent return vs. percent new) with the regression line used for the gold medal swimming data (year vs. time). In what ways are their interpretations similar and different?
How does the y-intercept of the regression line for life expectancy (Chapter 12) differ in interpretation from the y-intercept of a cost function (e.g., Aaron’s Word Processing Service)?
Compare the coefficient of determination for the CPI data (r² ≈ 0.756) to that of the third exam/final exam data (r² ≈ 0.44). What does each value tell us about the fit of the model?
Compare the practical meaning of a z-score of +2 versus a z-score of –2 for a medical measurement such as blood pressure or weight of a child.
How does the standard deviation σ serve different roles in the normal distribution formula compared to the standard deviation of residuals s in the regression model?
Compare the probability of a value being more than 2 standard deviations above the mean vs. more than 3 standard deviations above the mean in a normal distribution. How extreme are these events?
Compare the regression equation ŷ = –173.51 + 4.83x (with outlier) and ŷ = –355.19 + 7.39x (without outlier). What dramatic change occurred in both the slope and intercept?
How does the use of z-scores to compare SAT and ACT scores illustrate the broader concept of standardization? Compare this use to the standardization in a standard normal distribution.

Compare the assumption that “data are normally distributed” in the context of the normal distribution chapter to the regression assumption that “y values are normally distributed about the regression line.”
Compare a regression model for predicting sports stadium capacity (n=60) to a model for predicting checkout wait times (n=50). How does sample size affect confidence in the predictions?
Compare the concept of a “best-fit line” in the context of regression to the concept of the “mean” in descriptive statistics. In what sense are both measures of “center”?
Compare and contrast the practical usefulness of a high r value (e.g., 0.95) in a small dataset (n=5) versus a moderate r value (e.g., 0.50) in a large dataset (n=100).
How does the variability around the regression line (measured by s) compare to the overall variability in y (measured by sy)? What does a small s relative to sy tell us?

Compare the meaning of the y-intercept in the regression of fly ball distances (theoretical) to the regression of SCUBA diving depths and times (practical). When does the y-intercept have real-world meaning?
Compare using the Empirical Rule approximation to using the exact normal distribution formula (normalcdf). When would you prefer each approach?
How does the regression of the percent of hourly workers over time (Chapter 12) compare to the regression of life expectancy over time in terms of slope direction and interpretation?
Compare the calculation of the interquartile range (IQR) using the normal distribution (invNorm) with calculating it from raw data. What are the advantages and disadvantages of each method?
Compare the “direction” indicated by the sign of the correlation r with the “strength” indicated by the magnitude |r|. Can a strong negative correlation be more meaningful than a weak positive one?
Compare the sample correlation coefficient r as an estimate of ρ with other sample statistics (like x̄ estimating μ). What are the limitations of using a sample to estimate a population parameter?

Compare the regression results for coffee consumption vs. heart disease across 10 countries (Chapter 12) with the regression for weight vs. fuel efficiency. In what ways are these two analyses structurally similar despite having different variables?
How does the Pearson correlation coefficient r compare to the coefficient of determination r² in terms of what information each provides and which is more intuitive to interpret?

Compare the significance test for the correlation coefficient with a standard hypothesis test for a population mean. What elements do they share (null hypothesis, test statistic, p-value, conclusion), and where do they differ?

How does the concept of z-scores from the normal distribution chapter connect to the hypothesis testing of the correlation coefficient in Chapter 12? In what ways do both use standardization to make comparisons?

A student scores 73 on the third exam. Using the regression equation from Chapter 12 and the concept of prediction from Chapter 6, explain the full process of predicting their final exam score and quantifying the uncertainty in that prediction.

How does understanding the Empirical Rule (Chapter 6) help a researcher interpret the residuals of a regression model (Chapter 12)? Specifically, what percentage of residuals would you expect to fall within ±2s of the regression line?

In the NUMMI automobile example, the number of defective cars follows approximately a normal distribution. Explain how the concepts of z-scores and the Empirical Rule can be used together to identify unusually high or low defect rates in the quality control process.

The SAT and ACT are different tests with different scales. Using the concepts of z-scores (Chapter 6) and linear regression (Chapter 12), explain how a university admissions office could develop a unified model to predict college GPA from either test score.

How do the concepts of the coefficient of determination r² and the area under the normal distribution curve both express “proportion of something explained”? Develop an analogy that connects these two ideas.

In the regression of CPI over time, the textbook notes that although r is significant, a curve would be a better model. Using knowledge of both the normal distribution and regression concepts, explain what statistical signals suggest this, and what might happen to residuals if we force a linear model.

An ecologist is studying the sparrow hawk colony data. Integrate the concepts of outlier detection (Chapter 12), the meaning of residuals, and the normal distribution of errors to explain the full process of determining whether a data point is a true outlier versus natural variation.

How does the regression assumption that “y values are normally distributed about the line” (Chapter 12) directly connect to the properties of the normal distribution studied in Chapter 6? What would a violin plot of the y values at each x value look like if this assumption holds?

Using the concept of interpolation vs. extrapolation from Chapter 12 and the concept of the domain of a distribution from Chapter 6, explain why predictions outside the observed range of x values are unreliable even when r is significant.

How do the concepts of “sample size n” from both chapters (affecting z-scores in Chapter 6 and the significance of r in Chapter 12) work together to illustrate the fundamental statistical principle that larger samples give more reliable conclusions?

A medical researcher wants to predict systolic blood pressure (normally distributed with μ = 125, σ = 14) from body mass index (BMI). Integrate the z-score framework from Chapter 6 with the regression framework from Chapter 12 to describe the full statistical approach, including hypothesis testing.
